title: Chol HaMoed: The In-Between Days of Sukkot and Pesach
title_zh: 节中日:逾越节与住棚节的中间日子
title_he: חול המועד: הימים הביניים של סוכות ופסח
description: What's permitted and what's restricted during the intermediate days, the custom of outings and trips, and the special Torah readings.
author: Torah Light Team
cultural_parallels: Chinese philosophy - Daoism's recognition of transitions (dawn/dusk) having special character
I Ching (易经) - constant change and intermediate states having significance
Gradual scaling between extremes rather than binary sacred/profane
key_concepts: Chol HaMoed - intermediate days of Sukkot and Pesach
Work restrictions - permitted vs. prohibited categories
Tzorchei HaMoed - needs of the festival
Torah readings and Hallel during intermediate days
Custom of family outings and trips
Isru Chag - the day after the festival
Holiness as a spectrum, not binary

historical_periods.jewish_period: Spring and Autumn Period (development of Chol HaMoed distinction)
historical_periods.chinese_equivalent: Spring and Autumn Period (春秋时代)
Warring States to Eastern Han Dynasty (战国至东汉)

sources.description: Seven days eating unleavened bread, first and seventh days as sacred occasions
Sukkot - first day and eighth day as sacred occasions
Moses's request to see God's glory, Thirteen Attributes of Mercy
Source for Isru Chag - 'Bind the festival offering'
Read on Shabbat Chol HaMoed Pesach
'My creatures are drowning in the sea' - reason for Half Hallel on Pesach
Detailed discussions about permitted work on Chol HaMoed (compiled ~200 CE)

practical_applications: Understanding what work is permitted on Chol HaMoed
Planning family outings during intermediate days
Maintaining festive character while engaging in permitted activities
Attending synagogue for special Torah readings
Continuing to fulfill festival mitzvot (sukkah, matzah, etc.)
